old_claim.note: Joint-Stock Companies provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: The Board of Longitude was a public institution created under the 1714 Longitude Act, so parliamentary statute-making and commissioner appointment are the relevant governance substrate.

source_shape.source_locator: Collection introduction: the Board papers cover a public institution from 1714-1828; the 1714 Longitude Act nominated twenty-two Commissioners and assigned them official tasks to judge proposals, fund experiments, and reward successful schemes.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source identifies the Longitude Act and appointed Commissioners as the institutional basis for judging, funding, and rewarding longitude proposals.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Parliamentary statute-making matches a public prize board created by an Act; joint-stock companies are private capital-pooling institutions and are not evidenced as the prize-system substrate.

ontology_before: Longitude Prize Systems depended on joint-stock companies, implying corporate share-capital organization enabled the public reward board.
ontology_after: Longitude Prize Systems depend on parliaments and estates-general as the representative/statutory substrate for the 1714 Longitude Act and appointed Commissioners.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: longitude_prize_systems now depends on parliaments_estates_general and no longer directly depends on joint_stock_companies.
would_reject_if: The node is later narrowed to private subscription prizes rather than the Board created by the 1714 Longitude Act.
A stronger source shows joint-stock companies directly financed or governed the Board of Longitude.
A more precise parliamentary_statute_prize_systems node is added and replaces parliaments_estates_general as the governance intermediary.
short_reason: The Longitude Act and Commissioners are public statutory machinery, not joint-stock company machinery.
